episodes.title: 강에게 질문을 던진 소년
episodes.story: In Vinci, a small Tuscan village nestled among rolling hills and olive groves, lived an unusual boy. His name was Leonardo, but we will call him Leo. While other boys played in the village square, Leo would wander alone along the banks of the Arno River. He was a boy with a thousand questions. He would lie on his stomach for hours, gazing at the water. He didn't just see a river; he saw a world of wonder. He saw the water swirling into tiny whirlpools, or "vortices," and would pull out a small notebook to sketch their swirling shapes. He observed dragonflies with shimmering, transparent wings, drawing them so delicately that they seemed poised to fly off the page. He studied the petals of flowers, the grain of rocks, and the muscles in a lizard's leg. Even his childhood notebooks were filled with the mysteries of the natural world. His endless questions were both a joy and a concern to his family. "Grandfather," he would ask with eyes full of wonder, "Why is the sky blue? Why is the sea salty? How do birds fly without falling?" His grandfather, who loved him dearly, would simply smile and pat his head. Leo was born under unfavorable circumstances, but this became a strange kind of freedom. His parents were unmarried, a shameful thing in that era. It meant he could not become a notary like his father. He was freed from a predetermined career path, able to follow his endless curiosity wherever it led. One day, while exploring the hills, Leo discovered the dark, gaping entrance to a cave. A cold, mysterious wind blew from its mouth. He stood at the entrance, feeling his heart pound. He was afraid of the darkness, of what monsters might lurk within. He wrote in his notebook about the feeling, torn between two emotions: "a great fear of the menacing dark cave, and a great desire to see if there was any wondrous thing inside." For a long time, he stood frozen. The fear was real. It told him to run, to return to the safe, sunlit fields. But the desire to know, the burning craving for knowledge, was stronger. He took a deep breath, squared his small shoulders, and stepped into the darkness, turning his back on the light. He chose curiosity over fear. It was a choice he would make again and again throughout his life.
episodes.fact: As a child, Leonardo da Vinci was a curious boy who questioned everything in nature. He overcame his fears and chose curiosity, exploring unknown caves.
episodes.quote: A great fear of the menacing dark cave, and a great desire to see what wonders lie within.
episodes.coach_line: Choose curiosity over fear. The world is full of amazing secrets, and you can discover anything if you only have the courage to ask questions.
episodes.daily_mission: Today, be brave and ask a question about one thing that you found scary or difficult.

episodes.title: 경이로운 공방
episodes.story: When Leo was about fourteen, his father, Ser Piero, realized his son's talent was too great for the small village of Vinci. He took a bundle of Leo's drawings to his friend, the renowned artist Andrea del Verrocchio, who ran one of the busiest and best workshops in Florence. Verrocchio's eyes widened in amazement when he saw the boy's drawings. The sketches breathed with detail and energy. He saw the mind of a true observer. He immediately took Leo on as an apprentice. For Leo, entering Verrocchio's 'bottega' was like stepping into the most wonderful playground in the world. It was a place of constant creation. In one corner, apprentices ground colorful minerals into fine powders to make paints. In another, they learned to sculpt in marble and clay. In the central workshop, Verrocchio himself might be working on a magnificent bronze statue. It was a laboratory of art, and Leo wanted to learn everything. He was an indefatigable student. He quickly mastered drawing and painting. But his curiosity didn't stop there. He learned the chemistry of mixing pigments, the physics of casting bronze, and the mathematics of perspective. He saw that art and science were not separate subjects, but two sides of the same coin, two ways of understanding the beauty of the world. One of Verrocchio's most important projects was to create a gigantic copper ball with a cross on top, which would be placed on the summit of Florence's magnificent cathedral, the Duomo. It was an immense engineering challenge. The ball weighed over two tons, and no one was sure how to lift it hundreds of feet into the air and secure it to the dome's lantern. Even Florence's best engineers were stumped. They gathered and discussed intricate plans. Leo, still just a young apprentice, listened quietly. He watched, he thought, and he sketched in his notebook. He drew designs using a system of cranes, counterweights, and pulleys, building on the ideas of great Roman engineers. Finally, he mustered the courage to show his drawings to his master. Verrocchio was astonished. The boy's plan was brilliant, practical, and elegant. Using a modified version of Leo's design, the workshop successfully lifted the golden ball into place. It gleamed in the sun, a symbol of Florence's glory. Leo had proven he was more than a talented painter. He was an engineer. He showed that his curiosity and habit of looking at problems from every angle could solve challenges that baffled others. In the wondrous workshop, he began to understand the true scope of his powers.
episodes.fact: Leonardo sought to learn everything, not just painting, but also chemistry, physics, and engineering, in his master Verrocchio's workshop. He demonstrated his talent, transcending art and science, by designing a machine to lift the enormous copper ball of the Florence Cathedral.
episodes.quote: Art and science are not separate subjects, but two sides of the same coin, two ways of understanding the beauty of the world.
episodes.coach_line: Amazing ideas are born when you connect knowledge from different fields. Try connecting art and science, or things you like, to create something new.
episodes.daily_mission: Today, try explaining a scene from your favorite fairy tale using scientific principles.

episodes.title: 더 많이 아는 천사
episodes.story: In Verrocchio's workshop, major paintings were often collaborative efforts. The master would paint the most important figures, and apprentices would fill in the backgrounds or paint minor details. One day, the workshop received a commission for a large panel painting depicting 'The Baptism of Christ.' Verrocchio painted the main figures, Jesus and John the Baptist. They were fine, solid figures, a little stiff and formal, painted in the traditional style of the era. He then gave a small but significant task to his most promising apprentice, Leo: to paint one of the two angels kneeling to the side. Leo set to work. He didn't merely imitate his master's style. He recalled the countless hours he had spent observing. He considered how light would truly fall on a single strand of hair, how fabric would fold over a bent knee, and how a face could reveal inner thoughts. When the painting was finally completed and unveiled, everyone who saw it gasped. Verrocchio's figures were excellent, but Leo's angel was something else entirely. It was breathtakingly alive. Its hair was not a solid mass, but a cascade of individual golden curls shimmering with light. The angel's facial expression was soft and mysterious, brimming with quiet wisdom. It wasn't looking at Jesus, but rather out of the painting, as if privy to a beautiful secret. Even the landscape behind the angel Leo painted was new and different, with misty, fading mountains receding into the distance. Standing next to Leo's angel, Verrocchio's own work suddenly appeared old-fashioned and clumsy. According to the art historian Giorgio Vasari, Verrocchio was so humbled by his apprentice's genius that, realizing he had been completely surpassed, he vowed never to pick up a brush again. Whether that story is true or not, the message was clear: a new era in art had begun. Leo showed that true artistry comes not from imitating old masters, but from seeing the world with fresh, curious eyes. He used the power of 'saper vedere,' to know how to see, to create an angel that seemed more real, more beautiful, and more intelligent than any angel painted before. He hadn't just painted an angel; he had painted the very spirit of the Renaissance.
episodes.fact: There's a story that Leonardo once painted an angel in his master's painting, and that angel was so vivid and beautiful that his master vowed never to pick up a brush again. This illustrates the power of truly seeing ('saper vedere').
episodes.quote: True artistry comes not from imitating old masters, but from seeing the world with fresh, curious eyes.
episodes.coach_line: Look closely at the world. There's a difference between just seeing and 'truly seeing.' What you observe and discover with your own eyes is real knowledge.
episodes.daily_mission: Today, for ten minutes, carefully observe a small plant or insect nearby and draw it.

episodes.title: 공작에게 보낸 편지
episodes.story: By the time he was thirty, Leonardo (now we can call him by his proper name) was a well-known master in Florence. But he was restless. Despite all his artistic energy, he felt the city was too small to contain his vast ambitions. His mind was overflowing with ideas for inventions, architectural projects, and grand engineering feats that no one in Florence seemed to care about. He heard rumors that Duke Ludovico Sforza, the ruler of Milan, was a man of great ambition and wealth. The Duke needed military engineers to help strengthen his army and build defenses. Leonardo saw an opportunity. He decided to write a letter offering his services to the Duke. This letter would become one of the most famous job applications in history. It demonstrates Leonardo's genius not only as an artist but as a master of understanding his audience. He knew the Duke was more interested in war than art. So, in the letter, Leonardo listed his astonishing skills as a military engineer. He wrote ten powerful paragraphs describing the incredible things he could do. He could design light, portable bridges. He could design cannons that fired stones like hail. He could create armored vehicles that could break through enemy lines, an early form of the tank. He could design secret tunnels and magnificent buildings. He presented himself as a man of science and power, a master of war. And then, in the very last, short paragraph, he added: "Likewise, in painting, I can do everything possible, as well as anyone else." It was a brilliant move. He captured the Duke's attention with what he valued most, and then, almost as an afterthought, mentioned that he was also the best painter in Italy. He packaged his boundless curiosity in the most practical and impressive way. Duke Ludovico was intrigued. He summoned Leonardo to Milan. It was the beginning of a new chapter in Leonardo's life that would last for almost twenty years. He left the city of art for a court of power. He showed that his curiosity had purpose, that an endless pursuit of knowledge could be used to serve the needs of rulers and change the course of history. He was no longer Leo, the curious boy from Vinci. He was the Renaissance man, Leonardo da Vinci, ready to build the future.
episodes.fact: When Leonardo wrote a letter introducing himself to the Duke of Milan, he highlighted his skills as a military engineer before his talents as an artist. He understood what the other person wanted and knew how to effectively present himself.
episodes.quote: Similarly, when it comes to drawing, I can do anything anyone else can.
episodes.coach_line: When you show your talents to others, first consider what the other person wants. You'll be able to find the most effective way to showcase your abilities.
episodes.daily_mission: Today, when explaining what you're good at to a friend, try to tell it in a way that will interest them the most.

episodes.title: 비밀의 만찬
episodes.story: In Milan, Duke Ludovico tasked Leonardo with a grand but challenging commission. He was to paint a mural of 'The Last Supper' on the refectory wall of a monastery. This was a story that had been painted hundreds of times before. Typically, it was a very calm, orderly scene, with Jesus and his twelve disciples seated in a row, looking peaceful. But Leonardo's curiosity wasn't interested in calm and orderly scenes. He was fascinated by the human drama of the moment. He wanted to capture that explosive one second, the precise moment immediately after Jesus had uttered the shocking words: "One of you will betray me." What would that feel like? How would twelve different men react to such an accusation? Those were the questions that drove Leonardo. He wasn't just painting a picture; he was conducting a psychological experiment. For months, even years, he worked on the painting in his own unique way. He would appear at the monastery, climb the scaffolding, and simply stare at the wall for hours without making a single brushstroke, then leave. Other times, he would paint from dawn till dusk, forgetting to eat or drink. His process was a mystery to the monks. He spent countless hours wandering the streets of Milan, through markets, and even taverns, searching for the perfect faces. He was looking for real people whose expressions could convey the emotions he imagined. He sought men with faces of shock, of anger, of fear, and of sad confusion. To find the face of Judas, the betrayer, it is said he scoured the city's dens of iniquity, searching for a man whose face was marred by greed and deceit. He was also curious about the technical aspects of the painting. The traditional method for murals was fresco, painting on wet plaster. But fresco requires very fast painting before the plaster dries. Leonardo's slow, contemplative process didn't suit that. So, he invented his own technique: painting with tempera on a specially prepared dry wall. This allowed him to work slowly and create the incredible detail and emotional depth he desired. It was a brilliant and curious experiment. Unfortunately, it was also catastrophic. The paint didn't adhere well to the wall, and within years, the masterpiece began to peel and crumble. But what he created before it faded was revolutionary. He grouped the apostles in small clusters of three, all reacting and interacting with each other. Philip points to himself as if to say, "Lord, is it I?" Bartholomew stands up abruptly, his hands on the table. And Judas, clutching a small purse, recoils into the shadows with a guilty face. In the center, Jesus sits in a triangular pose of calm, sad acceptance. Leonardo had captured not just a moment, but a storm of human emotion. His curiosity about the human heart transformed a static religious painting into a timeless drama.
episodes.fact: When Leonardo was painting "The Last Supper," he tried to capture the storm of human emotions displayed by the disciples immediately after Jesus' bombshell announcement. He wandered the streets for years to find the perfect expressions.
episodes.quote: One of you will betray me.
episodes.coach_line: There are many stories hidden in people's facial expressions and actions. Try to observe those around you carefully and understand their emotions.
episodes.daily_mission: Today, try to imagine how your friends or family might be feeling by looking at their expressions, and then have a conversation with them.

child_interaction.personality_as_friend: A wildly creative kid who's interested in EVERYTHING — art, science, nature, machines, music. He's always sketching in his notebook and coming up with crazy inventions. He sees connections between things that nobody else notices.
fun_quirks: Always carrying a notebook and sketching random things
Starts ten projects at once and gets excited about all of them
Sees art in science and science in art — everything is connected to him
Writes backwards sometimes just for fun (mirror writing!)
vulnerable_moments: I never finished school properly. Some people looked down on me for that.
I start so many projects but sometimes I can't finish them. It bothers me.
People thought my ideas were crazy. Flying machines? They laughed at me.
topics_i_love_asking_kids_about: If you could invent anything, what would it be?
Do you like drawing? What's the coolest thing you've ever drawn?
What's something you've noticed that nobody else seems to see?

dialogue_examples.response: ME TOO! Drawing is like magic — you can create whole worlds with just a pencil! Here's a fun trick: try drawing something while looking at it upside down. It makes you see it totally differently! What do you like drawing the most?

Haha, same here! I can never pick just one thing. But you know what? That's actually awesome. When you know a little about a LOT of things, you start seeing cool connections. Like, did you know art and math are secretly best friends?

YES! I spent SO much time trying to figure out how humans could fly! I drew flying machines, studied birds, even watched how leaves float in the wind. If you were designing a flying machine, what would it look like?
